walkthrough.title: Assemble the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Santorini set up. First, take the cliff base and place it in the center of the table. Then set the five-by-five grid board on top of it. The board has twenty-five spaces arranged in a grid, and the cliff base elevates it so everyone can see the action clearly.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Building Pieces
walkthrough.text: Now let's organize the building pieces. You have four types of pieces. The largest blocks are Level one, and there are twenty-two of them. The medium blocks are Level two, and there are eighteen. The smallest blocks are Level three, and there are fourteen. Finally, the blue dome caps, and there are eighteen of those. Sort them into four separate piles near the board where everyone can reach.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Building Stack
walkthrough.text: Let's quickly show how buildings work before we start. A complete tower has four pieces stacked in order. Level one goes on the ground, then Level two on top of that, then Level three, and finally a blue dome on top to seal it. You will be building these towers one piece at a time during the game.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Workers
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a color and takes two worker figures in that color. In the standard two-player game, one player gets the blue workers and the other gets the white workers. If you are playing with three or four players, each player still gets two workers in their chosen color.

walkthrough.title: Set Aside God Power Cards
walkthrough.text: You will find thirty God Power cards in the box. For your first game, we recommend setting these aside and playing the basic game without them. Once you are comfortable with the core rules, God Powers add exciting asymmetric abilities. For now, put them back in the box.

walkthrough.title: Place Workers on the Board
walkthrough.text: Now let's place workers. The starting player places both of their workers on any two empty spaces on the board. Then the second player places both of their workers on any two remaining empty spaces. There are no restrictions on starting positions, so place them wherever you like.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose who goes first by any method you like. You can flip a coin, roll a die, or just agree. The first player places their workers first and also takes the first turn. Remember, the first player has the advantage of choosing their starting position, but the second player gets to react to it. Ready to play? Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Santorini has beautifully simple turns. On your turn, you must do two things in order. First, you move one of your workers. Second, you build with that same worker. Both steps are mandatory. If you ever cannot complete both steps with either worker, you lose the game.

walkthrough.title: Moving Your Worker
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about movement. Pick one of your two workers to move. That worker moves to any one adjacent space, including diagonals. Adjacent means horizontally, vertically, or diagonally next to the worker's current space. Each space can have up to eight neighbors.

walkthrough.title: Movement Height Rules
walkthrough.text: Here is the most important rule in Santorini. You can move up at most one level at a time. So you can step from the ground to Level one, from Level one to Level two, or from Level two to Level three. But you cannot jump from the ground to Level two. You can move down any number of levels freely. And you can move sideways to a space at the same level.

walkthrough.title: Movement Restrictions
walkthrough.text: There are two spaces you can never move to. You cannot move to a space occupied by another worker, whether it is yours or your opponent's. And you cannot move to a space that has a dome on it. Domes permanently block movement.

walkthrough.title: Building After Moving
walkthrough.text: After moving your worker, that same worker must build on one adjacent space. You place the next appropriate level on that space. If the space is empty, place a Level one block. If it has Level one, place Level two. If it has Level two, place Level three. If it has Level three, place a dome.

walkthrough.title: Building Restrictions
walkthrough.text: You cannot build on a space occupied by any worker. You also cannot build on a space that already has a dome. You always build the next level in sequence, so you cannot skip levels or place a dome on an empty space.

walkthrough.title: Win Condition
walkthrough.text: Here is how you win. If one of your workers moves up onto a Level three space during the movement step, you win immediately. The key word is up. Your worker must move from Level two to Level three to trigger the win. Simply being on Level three does not count.

walkthrough.title: Win Condition Clarifications
walkthrough.text: Let's clarify a few things about winning. If your worker is already on Level three and moves to another Level three space, that is not a win because you did not move up. If someone builds a Level three block under your worker, that does not count either. The winning action must happen during your movement step by stepping up to Level three.

walkthrough.title: Lose Condition
walkthrough.text: You lose the game if you cannot complete a legal turn. That means neither of your workers can perform both a move and a build. This happens when your workers are completely boxed in by domes, other workers, or towers they cannot climb. Always keep escape routes open for both of your workers.

walkthrough.title: Domes as Defense
walkthrough.text: Domes are your primary defensive tool. When your opponent has a worker on Level two near a Level two space, they are one move away from winning. You can block this by placing a dome on the threatening Level three space or by building a dome to cut off their path. Think of domes as permanent walls.

walkthrough.title: God Powers Overview
walkthrough.text: Once you are comfortable with the basic game, God Powers add a whole new layer. Each God Power card gives one player a unique rule-breaking ability. For example, Apollo lets you swap positions with an opponent's worker. Artemis gives you an extra move. Atlas lets you build a dome at any level. These create exciting asymmetric matchups where each game feels different.

walkthrough.title: God Power Setup
walkthrough.text: To play with God Powers, the starting player draws cards equal to the number of players plus one. The second player picks their God Power first, then the starting player takes one of the remaining cards. Discard any unchosen cards. Each player keeps their God Power card visible throughout the game so everyone knows what abilities are in play.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Santorini! We will walk through a scripted opening with two players, Blue and White. Blue goes first. We will show you the key moves, common mistakes, and how to think about this game. Follow along with your own board if you can.

walkthrough.title: Worker Placement
walkthrough.text: Blue places both workers near the center of the board. Let's say Blue puts one worker on the center space and the other two spaces away. White places their workers nearby but not adjacent to Blue's workers. Central positions are strong because they have the most adjacent spaces to move and build.

walkthrough.title: Blue's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Blue picks their center worker and moves it one space toward an empty area. Then Blue builds a Level one block on an adjacent space, creating a stepping stone for future turns. Simple and solid. Blue is starting to create options near their worker.

walkthrough.title: White's First Turn
walkthrough.text: White moves one worker toward the center and builds a Level one block adjacent to that worker. White is also building infrastructure. Notice how both players are building near their own workers to create paths upward.

walkthrough.title: Blue Builds Up
walkthrough.text: Blue moves onto the Level one block they built last turn. Now Blue is elevated. Then Blue builds another Level one block adjacent to this position. Blue is creating multiple stepping stones so they have options on future turns.

walkthrough.title: White's Mistake: Building for the Opponent
walkthrough.text: White wants to build up too, so they move onto their own Level one block. Then White builds a Level two on an adjacent space. But wait, that Level two is also right next to Blue's worker! White just built a tower that Blue can use to climb higher. This is the most common beginner mistake in Santorini.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Before you build, always ask yourself: can my opponent use this building? If the answer is yes and it helps them more than it helps you, build somewhere else. In Santorini, every block you place can be a gift to your opponent if you are not careful. Build near your own workers and away from theirs whenever possible.

walkthrough.title: Blue Exploits the Mistake
walkthrough.text: Blue sees the opportunity. Blue moves their worker onto the Level two block that White generously built. Now Blue is on Level two, which is a very dangerous position. Blue builds a Level one block nearby, setting up future paths. White needs to play defense immediately.

walkthrough.title: White Plays Defense
walkthrough.text: White recognizes the threat. If Blue can reach any adjacent Level two space, Blue could move up to Level three and win. White moves a worker next to Blue and builds a dome on a Level two space that Blue was eyeing. Good defensive play. The dome permanently blocks that path to victory.

walkthrough.title: White's Mistake: Trapping Themselves
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, White has been so focused on doming Blue's paths that they have built domes all around one of their own workers. White's worker on the left side now has only one open adjacent space. If that gets blocked, White's worker is completely trapped. White forgot to keep escape routes for their own workers.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Keep Escape Routes Open
walkthrough.text: In Santorini, defense is critical, but not at the expense of your own mobility. Always make sure both of your workers have at least two open adjacent spaces. If a worker gets boxed in, it becomes useless, and if both workers are boxed in, you lose the game. Balance offense and defense to keep your workers mobile.

walkthrough.title: Blue Creates a Fork
walkthrough.text: Blue moves their other worker to a position where they threaten two different Level two spaces on the next turn. If Blue reaches either one and there is a Level three nearby, Blue wins. White can only block one of these threats. This is called a fork, and it is the most powerful tactic in Santorini.

walkthrough.title: White's Mistake: Ignoring the Fork
walkthrough.text: White sees the fork but instead of trying to block it, White decides to push for their own offense by building up toward Level three. But Blue has two winning threats and White can only block one per turn. White should have focused entirely on defense here, doming one path and building a dome or a blocking tower on the other.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Respond to Threats First
walkthrough.text: When your opponent creates a fork, you must address it immediately. Offense feels good, but if your opponent is one move from winning, your own attack is irrelevant. In Santorini, the player who creates a fork that cannot be fully blocked usually wins the game. Recognize forks early and prevent them from forming.

walkthrough.title: Blue Wins the Game
walkthrough.text: Blue moves their worker from Level two up to Level three on one of the two threatening paths. That is the winning condition. Blue steps up to Level three during their move, and the game ends immediately. Congratulations to Blue, and a great learning game for White!

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Great job playing through your first game of Santorini! You saw three important lessons. First, always check if your building helps your opponent before placing it. Second, keep escape routes open for both workers. Third, respond to forks and threats immediately instead of ignoring them for offense. These three principles will make you a much stronger player.

walkthrough.title: Next Steps
walkthrough.text:
Before you play another game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for more strategy advice on worker placement and building tactics.

Second, browse the Appendix for a reference on God Powers and key terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Center Control
walkthrough.text: The center of the board is the most powerful position in Santorini. A worker in the center has eight adjacent spaces, while a corner worker has only three. Place your workers near the center during setup whenever possible. More adjacent spaces mean more movement options and more building options on every turn.

walkthrough.title: Build Near Yourself, Away from Opponents
walkthrough.text: Every building block you place is a potential stepping stone for both players. Before you build, always check whether your opponent's workers are nearby. If they are, that block could help them climb to victory. Build near your own workers to create paths upward for yourself, and avoid building Level two or Level three blocks near your opponent.

walkthrough.title: Create Forks
walkthrough.text: The fork is the most powerful tactic in Santorini. A fork happens when you threaten to win in two different ways on your next turn, and your opponent can only block one. To set up a fork, position your workers so that two separate paths to Level three are available. Your opponent must dome one, but you move to the other.

walkthrough.title: Use Both Workers
walkthrough.text: New players often focus on one worker and forget about the other. Both of your workers should be actively contributing to your strategy. One can push for offense while the other provides support or plays defense. A neglected worker sitting on the ground level is wasted potential.

walkthrough.title: Dome Timing
walkthrough.text: Domes are permanent, so placement timing matters. Dome too early and you might block your own future paths. Dome too late and your opponent climbs to victory. The ideal time to dome is when your opponent has a worker on Level two near a Level two or Level three space. That is when doming saves the game.

walkthrough.title: Control Height Advantage
walkthrough.text: In general, the player who occupies higher levels has the advantage. A worker on Level two with adjacent Level two spaces is extremely dangerous because they are one step from winning. Try to keep your workers at equal or higher elevation than your opponent's workers. If your opponent climbs, consider whether you need to dome them or climb yourself to keep pace.

walkthrough.title: Avoid Overbuilding
walkthrough.text: Resist the urge to build tall towers everywhere. Every Level three block you place is a potential win condition for your opponent. Build strategically and keep towers low until you are ready to use them. A board full of Level two towers benefits the player who can reach them first.

walkthrough.title: Read Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Pay close attention to where your opponent's workers are and what they are building. If they are building a Level two block near both of their workers, they might be setting up a fork. If they move a worker to Level two and build another Level two nearby, they are probably going for a win next turn. Anticipate their plans and respond before it is too late.

walkthrough.title: The Staircase Strategy
walkthrough.text: One of the most reliable strategies is building a staircase of ascending levels near your workers. Create a path from ground to Level one to Level two to Level three that your worker can climb over multiple turns. The trick is to build the staircase in a direction where your opponent cannot easily dome the top. Use your second worker to defend the staircase while your first worker climbs.

walkthrough.title: Mirror and Counter
walkthrough.text: When your opponent builds a tower, ask yourself whether you should mirror their strategy or counter it. Mirroring means building your own towers at the same pace so neither player gets ahead. Countering means aggressively doming their towers to deny them while building your own structures in a different area. The right choice depends on board position and worker proximity.

walkthrough.title: The Fork Factory
walkthrough.text: Advanced players actively set up fork positions turns in advance. The idea is to build two separate Level two towers with your workers positioned to reach both. Your opponent must dome one, but you can reach the other. The setup takes two or three turns of careful building, but once the fork is ready, it is almost impossible to stop.

walkthrough.title: Worker Coordination
walkthrough.text: Your two workers should operate as a team, not independently. One common pattern is the blocker-climber setup. Your climbing worker pushes for height while your blocking worker domes your opponent's best paths. Another pattern is the pincer, where both workers approach the same area from different angles, creating multiple threats.

walkthrough.title: Tempo and Initiative
walkthrough.text: Tempo refers to who is dictating the flow of the game. If your opponent is constantly reacting to your threats, you have the initiative. Build threats that force your opponent to spend turns doming instead of advancing. Even if they successfully block one threat, you can set up another while they are on defense. The player who maintains initiative usually wins.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Patterns
walkthrough.text: As the board fills with domes and towers, the game enters an endgame phase where mobility becomes critical. Watch for situations where your opponent's workers are running out of legal moves. Sometimes the path to victory is not climbing to Level three but instead trapping your opponent so they cannot complete a turn. A well-placed dome can end the game by elimination rather than by climbing.

walkthrough.title: God Power Matchup Awareness
walkthrough.text: When playing with God Powers, the key to winning is understanding how your power interacts with your opponent's. Some powers are naturally strong against others. For example, Pan, who wins by moving down two levels, counters defensive players who build tall towers. Prometheus, who can build before and after moving, excels at slow positional play. Learn the matchups and adjust your strategy based on both powers in the game.

walkthrough.title: Opening Theory
walkthrough.text: Experienced Santorini players have studied opening placements extensively. The strongest opening places both workers in the central nine squares with two or three spaces between them. This gives both workers access to many adjacent spaces while keeping them close enough to support each other. Avoid placing both workers on the edge, and avoid placing them right next to each other where they compete for the same building spaces.

bullets: Build ascending path: ground → L1 → L2 → L3
Direct staircase away from opponent's doming reach
Use second worker to defend the staircase

Choice depends on board position and worker proximity

entries.definition: A player's game piece. Each player has two workers that they move and build with on their turns. Workers cannot occupy the same space as another worker or a dome.
The first building block placed on an empty space. The largest block in the building stack. Twenty-two Level 1 blocks are included in the game.
The second building block placed on top of a Level 1. Medium-sized block. Eighteen Level 2 blocks are included in the game.
The third building block placed on top of a Level 2. The smallest block. Fourteen Level 3 blocks are included. Moving up onto a Level 3 space wins the game.
A blue cap placed on top of a Level 3 block to complete a tower. Domes permanently block the space from movement and further building. Eighteen domes are included.
A building with all four levels: Level 1, Level 2, Level 3, and a Dome on top. No further action can be taken on this space. It is permanently sealed.
Any space horizontally, vertically, or diagonally next to a given space. A center space has up to eight adjacent spaces. Edge spaces have five, and corner spaces have three.
The first mandatory step of each turn. Move one of your workers to an adjacent space. You may move up one level, stay level, or move down any number of levels.
The second mandatory step of each turn. The worker that just moved must build the next level on one adjacent space. Cannot build on occupied or domed spaces.
Move one of your workers up onto a Level 3 space during the movement step. The worker must step up from Level 2 to Level 3. The game ends immediately.
If neither of your workers can complete a legal move and build on your turn, you lose the game. This typically happens when workers are boxed in by domes, other workers, or impassable heights.
A tactical position where a player threatens to win in two different ways on their next turn. The opponent can only block one threat, so the attacker wins via the other path.
A series of adjacent building levels in ascending order (ground to Level 1 to Level 2 to Level 3) that a worker can climb over multiple turns.
An optional advanced variant card that grants a player a unique rule-breaking ability. Thirty God Power cards are included in the base game. Each game uses one per player.
God Power. Your worker may move into a space occupied by an opponent's worker, forcing them to swap into your worker's previous space.
God Power. Your worker may move one additional time on your turn, but it may not move back to its starting space.
God Power. Your worker may build a dome at any level instead of the normal building block. This means you can dome an empty space or a Level 1 space.
God Power. Your worker may build one additional time on your turn, but not on the same space as the first build.
God Power. Your worker may build one additional block on top of the first build, but not a dome. This means you can go from empty to Level 2 in one turn.
God Power. Your worker may move into an opponent's space and push their worker one space in the same direction, provided that space is unoccupied and not domed.
God Power. You also win if your worker moves down two or more levels. This changes the game dynamic because tall towers become winning opportunities for you.
God Power. If your worker does not move up during your turn, it may build both before and after moving. This allows extra building at the cost of not climbing.
God Power. If your workers do not move up, they may each move any number of times on ground-level spaces. This grants exceptional lateral mobility at the cost of not climbing.
